past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed 5 years ago, on rate control only; patient discontinued warfarin 6 months ago due to gingival bleeding
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled, diagnosed 4 years ago
Gestational diabetes during two pregnancies (ages 34 and 38)
COVID-19 infection 8 weeks ago with mild respiratory symptoms, managed at home, unvaccinated

medications.indication: Atrial fibrillation rate control and hypertension

family_history: Mother had ischemic stroke at age 72
Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension, died of heart failure at age 79
Sister with deep vein thrombosis at age 55

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in an apartment; daughter lives nearby
social_history.exercise: Walks 15 minutes daily, limited by knee pain

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to self and place but not to date. Expressive aphasia: limited verbal output with telegraphic speech, intact comprehension for simple commands. Unable to name low-frequency objects. Able to follow two-step commands with left hand. Reading comprehension impaired for complex sentences.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Rightward gaze preference; incomplete left gaze on oculocephalic maneuver. Right lower facial droop with forehead sparing. Gag reflex intact. Tongue protrusion midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: flaccid weakness, 1/5 throughout. Right lower extremity: 2/5 hip flexion, 1/5 at ankle dorsiflexion. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. Right arm pronator drift with immediate fall to bed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased pinprick and light touch sensation over right face, arm, and leg. Right-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation. Proprioception reduced in right hand.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 2+. Right upper extremity 3+, right lower extremity 3+ with extensor plantar response on the right (positive Babinski sign). Left plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess right side due to significant weakness. Left finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact without dysmetria.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate due to right-sided hemiparesis.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 16. Right visual field cut to confrontation. No meningismus. No signs of increased intracranial pressure.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Elena Vasquez is a 68-year-old right-handed woman with a history of atrial fibrillation (previously on warfarin, self-discontinued 6 months ago), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and recent COVID-19 infection approximately 8 weeks ago, who was brought to the emergency department by her daughter after acute onset of right-sided weakness and speech difficulty. The daughter reports that she spoke with her mother on the phone at 09:00 this morning and she sounded entirely normal. When the daughter arrived at her mother's apartment at 11:15 for a planned lunch, she found her sitting on the kitchen floor, leaning against the cabinets. The patient was attempting to speak but producing only fragmented words. Her right arm was limp at her side and she was unable to stand. There was a broken plate on the floor suggesting she had dropped it. The daughter called 911 immediately. EMS arrived at 11:28 and documented right facial droop, right hemiparesis, and expressive aphasia. A prehospital stroke alert was activated. Field glucose was 142 mg/dL. The patient was transported emergently to this comprehensive stroke center, arriving at 11:52. On the cardiac monitor, she was noted to be in atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response. The patient appeared frightened and was producing short phrases with significant word-finding difficulty but could nod appropriately to simple yes-or-no questions. There was no reported preceding headache, seizure activity, recent head trauma, or fever. The daughter confirms that her mother had COVID-19 approximately 8 weeks ago with cough and fatigue lasting about 10 days, managed entirely at home without hospitalization. She had not been vaccinated against SARS-CoV-2. The daughter also confirms her mother stopped taking warfarin about 6 months ago because of bleeding gums and had not followed up with her cardiologist since. Last known well time is established at 09:00, approximately 2 hours and 52 minutes prior to ED arrival.

findings.location: Left middle cerebral artery territory involving the left frontal operculum (Broca area), left insular cortex, left basal ganglia (caudate head and putamen), and adjacent corona radiata
findings.size: Approximately 4.8 x 3.5 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity suggesting onset greater than 3 hours
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in left MCA territory

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement in the left frontal region. No midline shift. Left lateral ventricle mildly compressed.
findings.borders: Well-defined margins conforming to left MCA superior division territory

signal_characteristics.MRA: Abrupt cutoff of flow signal in the left MCA at the proximal M1 segment with absent distal flow
signal_characteristics.TOF: Loss of flow-related enhancement in the left MCA distribution

additional_observations: Moderate periventricular and subcortical white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2)
Right MCA and bilateral ACA and PCA are patent on MRA
Old lacunar infarct in right basal ganglia, clinically silent based on history
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss consistent with age
mri.impression: 1. Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion involving the left frontal operculum, insular cortex, basal ganglia, and corona radiata. 2. Left MCA M1 segment occlusion on MRA consistent with large vessel occlusion. 3. No hemorrhagic transformation. 4. Old right basal ganglia lacunar infarct. 5. Chronic small vessel ischemic disease. Urgent neurology consultation for IV thrombolysis and/or mechanical thrombectomy is recommended.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic infarction, left MCA territory
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction with ADC confirmation in vascular territory distribution with M1 occlusion on MRA
No intraparenchymal hemorrhage on any sequence

recommended_actions: Urgent neurology and neurointerventional consultation for IV tPA and mechanical thrombectomy
CTA head and neck for further vascular characterization
Follow-up imaging in 24 hours to assess for hemorrhagic transformation

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 > 35 mm)
T-wave inversions in leads V4-V6, likely strain pattern
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 112 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria with strain pattern. No acute ischemic changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation is the most likely cardioembolic source. Rapid ventricular response warrants rate control. LVH with strain pattern consistent with longstanding hypertension.

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known diabetes and acute stress hyperglycemia

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Normal INR confirms patient is not anticoagulated

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Above target for patient with multiple vascular risk factors

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, may reflect demand ischemia in setting of AF with RVR and acute stroke
Suboptimally controlled diabetes, significant stroke risk factor
Elevated; may be related to recent COVID-19 infection, acute stroke, or underlying atrial fibrillation. Does not indicate active anticoagulation.
Mildly elevated; may reflect post-COVID inflammatory state or acute stroke-related inflammation

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results reveal stress hyperglycemia consistent with known diabetes and acute stroke, suboptimal lipid panel despite statin therapy, and mildly elevated troponin likely representing demand ischemia. Coagulation studies are normal confirming the patient is not anticoagulated. HbA1c of 7.2% indicates suboptimally controlled diabetes. D-dimer is elevated, which may be related to the recent COVID-19 infection or the acute stroke itself. CRP is mildly elevated, consistent with a post-COVID inflammatory state or acute stroke-related inflammation.

findings.location: Left MCA at the M1 segment

signal_characteristics.CTA: Complete occlusion of the left MCA at the proximal M1 segment with absent distal opacification. No ICA terminus involvement.
Mixed calcified and soft plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations. Right ICA approximately 30% stenosis. Left ICA approximately 40% stenosis.

additional_observations: Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with normal caliber
Circle of Willis: bilateral A1 segments patent, fetal-type right PCA
output.impression: 1. Left MCA M1 segment occlusion consistent with large vessel occlusion amenable to mechanical thrombectomy. 2. Mild-to-moderate bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerosis. 3. No cervical vessel dissection.
recommended_actions: Consider IV alteplase as bridge to thrombectomy if within time window
CT perfusion to assess ischemic penumbra if treatment decision requires further data

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Moderately dilated left atrium, consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation
Likely functional, due to atrial dilation and annular enlargement

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 52%), concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with chronic hypertension, and moderate left atrial dilation consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation. Mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation, likely functional. No intracardiac thrombus visualized; TEE may be considered for definitive left atrial appendage evaluation. Findings support a cardioembolic source in the setting of atrial fibrillation with structural heart disease.

findings: Continuous atrial fibrillation throughout the 24-hour recording
No periods of sinus rhythm identified
Rare premature ventricular complexes (38 in 24 hours, <1% burden)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
output.interpretation: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals persistent atrial fibrillation with rate control that is borderline adequate on current medications (average rate 94 bpm). No pauses or ventricular arrhythmias of concern.
output.clinical_correlation: Persistent atrial fibrillation confirmed, underscoring the clear indication for systemic anticoagulation for secondary stroke prevention once acute phase management is complete.

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel is entirely within normal limits. No hereditary or acquired thrombophilia identified. Negative antiphospholipid antibodies and lupus anticoagulant. The stroke etiology is most likely cardioembolic given known atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation and the absence of a prothrombotic disorder.

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries with normal flow velocities
Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with antegrade flow
Heterogeneous plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations, predominantly calcified
output.impression: 1. Mild-to-moderate (40-49%) stenosis of the left internal carotid artery. 2. Mild (<40%) stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. 3. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. Carotid stenosis is not at a surgical threshold; primary stroke mechanism is cardioembolic given atrial fibrillation.
recommended_actions: Medical optimization of atherosclerotic risk factors
Repeat carotid imaging in 6-12 months to monitor progression
Anticoagulation for AF-related cardioembolic stroke prevention takes priority over antiplatelet therapy

output.query: COVID-19 SARS-CoV-2 ischemic stroke risk post-infection thromboinflammatory

results.title: Risk of ischemic stroke in patients with COVID-19 vs patients with influenza
results.authors: Merkler AE, Parikh NS, et al.
results.key_finding: COVID-19 was associated with a 7.6-fold increased odds of ischemic stroke compared with influenza, even after adjustment for vascular risk factors.

results.title: Long-term cardiovascular outcomes of COVID-19
results.authors: Xie Y, Xu E, et al.
results.key_finding: People who had COVID-19 had increased risk of cerebrovascular disorders including ischemic stroke up to 12 months after infection, with hazard ratio 1.52 for stroke.

results.title: Endotheliopathy and platelet dysfunction as hallmarks of fatal COVID-19
results.authors: Goshua G, Pine AB, et al.
results.key_finding: COVID-19 causes significant endothelial injury and platelet activation, creating a prothrombotic state that may persist beyond the acute infection phase.

output.summary: SARS-CoV-2 infection is associated with a significantly increased risk of ischemic stroke, both during the acute infection and for up to 12 months afterward. The mechanism involves endotheliopathy, platelet dysfunction, and a hypercoagulable/thromboinflammatory state. Patients with pre-existing cardiovascular risk factors such as atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and diabetes are at compounded risk. However, in patients with known AF not on anticoagulation, the primary stroke mechanism remains cardioembolic, and COVID-19 may act as an additional precipitant rather than the sole cause.

interactions: Aspirin 81 mg daily: Increased bleeding risk when combined with thrombolytics. However, aspirin is NOT a contraindication to IV alteplase per AHA/ASA guidelines. Aspirin should be held for 24 hours after alteplase administration.
contraindications: Patient is NOT currently on anticoagulants (INR 1.0, warfarin discontinued 6 months ago) - no contraindication from anticoagulation
No known coagulopathy - PTT and PT within normal limits
warnings: Diltiazem: No direct interaction with alteplase, but monitor blood pressure closely as calcium channel blockers may affect hemodynamics
Metformin: Should be held if contrast studies are anticipated due to renal risk, but no interaction with alteplase
Monitor for angioedema, especially in patients on ACE inhibitors (patient is not on ACE inhibitor, on amlodipine instead)
alternatives: Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg IV single bolus (non-inferior to alteplase per AcT trial, simpler administration, particularly in bridging-to-thrombectomy setting)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left middle cerebral artery territory, cardioembolic etiology secondary to atrial fibrillation

differential.key_distinguishing: CT/MRI shows no hemorrhage; DWI restriction in vascular territory confirms ischemic etiology
No seizure activity witnessed, no history of epilepsy, imaging confirms acute infarction with large vessel occlusion
Age 68 with no migraine history, persistent deficits, and imaging confirmation of ischemic infarction

Although recent COVID-19 may have contributed to a prothrombotic state, the primary mechanism is cardioembolic given established AF without anticoagulation. Hypercoagulability panel is negative for acquired thrombophilia. COVID-19 infection was 8 weeks prior.
Field glucose 142 mg/dL and ED glucose 172 mg/dL; hypoglycemia is ruled out

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and perform rapid triage including last known well time, NIHSS assessment, and fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 16, LKW approximately 2h52m prior, glucose 142 mg/dL in field

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to rule out hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hemorrhage; possible early ischemic changes in left MCA territory

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, troponin, and glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal coagulation confirming patient not anticoagulated, stress hyperglycemia, elevated D-dimer and CRP
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response confirming cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Administer IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg), 10% as bolus and remainder over 60 minutes, given patient is within the 4.5-hour window
Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion and candidacy for thrombectomy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left MCA M1 occlusion confirmed, mild bilateral carotid atherosclerosis

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurointerventional surgery for emergent mechanical thrombectomy given large vessel occlusion
Obtain brain MRI with DWI to confirm infarct extent and assess for hemorrhagic transformation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion, left MCA M1 occlusion on MRA

optimal_actions.action: Manage blood pressure permissively (target <185/110 pre-tPA, then <180/105 for 24 hours post-tPA)

Initiate anticoagulation with DOAC (e.g., apixaban 5 mg BID or rivaroxaban 20 mg daily) for secondary stroke prevention once acute phase is stable and hemorrhagic transformation excluded, typically 4-14 days post-stroke
Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate cardiac structure and function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dilated left atrium, LVH, mildly reduced EF consistent with AF-related cardiomyopathy

optimal_actions.action: Admit to neurological ICU or dedicated stroke unit for close monitoring

critical_actions: Establish last known well time to determine thrombolysis eligibility
Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage before thrombolysis
Administer IV alteplase within the 4.5-hour treatment window
Identify large vessel occlusion and consult for mechanical thrombectomy
Obtain ECG to identify atrial fibrillation as the stroke mechanism
Initiate anticoagulation for secondary stroke prevention once safe to do so
Monitor in ICU or stroke unit with serial neurological assessments
contraindicated_actions: Administering anticoagulation (heparin, DOAC) in the acute phase before excluding hemorrhagic transformation
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute phase (risk of worsening ischemia in penumbral tissue)
Administering antiplatelet agents concurrently with IV alteplase within 24 hours
Delaying IV alteplase to obtain MRI in a patient within the treatment window
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of acute large territory stroke with mass effect
Administering IV alteplase if INR were elevated above 1.7
key_reasoning_points: Sudden onset focal neurological deficit (right hemiparesis, expressive aphasia) with last known well within 4.5 hours defines an acute stroke presentation eligible for IV thrombolysis
Known atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation (warfarin self-discontinued 6 months ago) is the most likely cardioembolic mechanism, confirmed by ECG showing AF with rapid ventricular response
MRI demonstrates classic acute ischemic stroke: DWI hyperintensity with ADC hypointensity in the left MCA territory with M1 occlusion on MRA, consistent with large vessel occlusion
Normal INR confirms the patient is not anticoagulated, making her eligible for IV alteplase and reinforcing the clinical history of warfarin discontinuation

Recent COVID-19 infection (8 weeks prior) may have contributed to a prothrombotic and inflammatory state (elevated D-dimer and CRP), but the primary mechanism remains cardioembolic from AF. Hypercoagulability workup is negative for antiphospholipid syndrome and inherited thrombophilia
Large vessel occlusion (left MCA M1) mandates consideration of mechanical thrombectomy in addition to IV thrombolysis (bridging therapy)
Multiple modifiable risk factors (AF, hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, obesity) compound stroke risk and must be addressed for secondary prevention

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic presentation with sudden onset right hemiparesis and expressive aphasia in a patient with known atrial fibrillation not on anticoagulation. Clear large vessel occlusion on imaging. Recent COVID-19 adds clinical context but does not obscure the primary diagnosis.
